questions.stem: A 6-month-old male infant is brought to the emergency department with tachypnea, hepatomegaly, and peripheral edema. Echocardiography reveals a large ventricular septal defect with left-to-right shunting. Over the next several years, the child develops progressive exercise intolerance, worsening cyanosis, and digital clubbing. Repeat echocardiography now demonstrates right-to-left shunting across the defect. Which of the following best describes the sequence of pathophysiologic events that led to this patient's current condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Left-to-right shunting caused pulmonary overcirculation, leading to irreversible pulmonary vascular remodeling and reversal of the shunt direction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Right ventricular dilation from volume overload caused tricuspid regurgitation, leading to systemic venous congestion and paradoxical embolization

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chronic left ventricular volume overload caused progressive left ventricular failure, resulting in reversal of the interventricular pressure gradient

choices.label: D
choices.text: Progressive fibrosis of the ventricular septum surrounding the defect caused narrowing of the right ventricular outflow tract and increased right-sided pressures

choices.label: E
choices.text: Recurrent pulmonary infections from increased pulmonary blood flow caused parenchymal destruction and secondary right ventricular pressure overload

explanation.summary: This patient developed Eisenmenger syndrome, in which a long-standing left-to-right shunt causes progressive pulmonary hypertension through irreversible pulmonary vascular remodeling, eventually raising right ventricular pressure above left ventricular pressure and reversing the shunt to right-to-left.
explanation.whyCorrect: Eisenmenger syndrome follows a defined sequence: pulmonary artery overload from left-to-right shunting leads to pulmonary hypertension, then RV hypertrophy, then RV pressure exceeding LV pressure, and finally shunt reversal to right-to-left with resultant cyanosis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: While tricuspid regurgitation can occur, the mechanism of shunt reversal in Eisenmenger syndrome is driven by pulmonary vascular remodeling and increased pulmonary pressures, not by tricuspid valve dysfunction or paradoxical embolization.
whyWrongByChoice.C: In left-to-right shunts, it is the right ventricle and pulmonary vasculature that bear the excess volume load. The shunt reversal occurs because right-sided pressures rise above left-sided pressures due to pulmonary vascular disease, not left ventricular failure.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The mechanism of Eisenmenger syndrome involves pulmonary vascular remodeling, not fibrosis around the septal defect. Right ventricular outflow tract narrowing is a feature of tetralogy of Fallot, not a complication of isolated VSD.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Although increased pulmonary blood flow can predispose to respiratory infections, Eisenmenger syndrome results from progressive pulmonary vascular remodeling and hypertension, not from parenchymal lung destruction due to infection.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman with a history of alcohol use disorder delivers a full-term infant. The newborn is noted to have microcephaly, smooth philtrum, and a thin upper lip. Echocardiography reveals a cardiac defect. Based on the teratogenic exposure, which of the following congenital heart defects is LEAST likely to be found in this infant?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Atrial septal defect with left-to-right shunting across the interatrial septum

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ventricular septal defect with a holosystolic murmur at the left sternal border

choices.label: C
choices.text: Patent ductus arteriosus with a continuous machine-like murmur

choices.label: D
choices.text: Transposition of the great arteries with the aorta arising from the right ventricle

choices.label: E
choices.text: Tetralogy of Fallot with right ventricular outflow tract obstruction

explanation.summary: Fetal alcohol syndrome is associated with VSD, ASD, PDA, and tetralogy of Fallot. Transposition of the great arteries is associated with maternal diabetes, not fetal alcohol exposure.
explanation.whyCorrect: Transposition of the great arteries is linked to maternal diabetes as a risk factor, not prenatal alcohol exposure. It results from failure of the aorticopulmonary septum to spiral during development, which is not a recognized consequence of alcohol teratogenicity.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Atrial septal defect is one of the four congenital heart defects specifically associated with fetal alcohol syndrome, making it a likely finding in this infant.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Ventricular septal defect is associated with fetal alcohol exposure and is also the most common congenital heart defect overall, making it a likely finding.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Patent ductus arteriosus is one of the recognized cardiac defects associated with prenatal alcohol exposure and fetal alcohol syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tetralogy of Fallot is one of the four congenital heart defects specifically linked to fetal alcohol exposure, making it a plausible finding in this infant.

questions.stem: A newborn is cyanotic immediately after delivery. Physical examination reveals a single loud S2 and a systolic ejection murmur. Echocardiography demonstrates a single arterial trunk arising from both ventricles, supplying the systemic, pulmonary, and coronary circulations. Which of the following embryologic mechanisms best explains this cardiac malformation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Failure of the tricuspid valve to develop during cardiac septation in the embryonic period

choices.label: B
choices.text: Misalignment of the outflow tract septum causing overriding of the aorta with right ventricular outflow obstruction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Failure of the aorticopulmonary septum to form, leaving the outflow tract undivided

choices.label: D
choices.text: Failure of the aorticopulmonary septum to spiral during outflow tract development

choices.label: E
choices.text: Failure of the pulmonary veins to establish normal connections with the left atrium

explanation.summary: Persistent truncus arteriosus results from failure of the aorticopulmonary septum to form, leaving a single undivided outflow tract that serves both ventricles.
explanation.whyCorrect: In persistent truncus arteriosus, the aorticopulmonary septum fails to develop entirely, resulting in one common outflow tract for both ventricles that supplies systemic, pulmonary, and coronary circulations.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Failure of the tricuspid valve to develop describes the embryologic basis of tricuspid atresia, not persistent truncus arteriosus. Tricuspid atresia results in absent communication between the right atrium and right ventricle.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Misalignment of the outflow tract septum describes the embryologic basis of tetralogy of Fallot, which involves VSD, overriding aorta, pulmonary stenosis, and right ventricular hypertrophy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Failure of the aorticopulmonary septum to spiral describes the embryologic basis of transposition of the great arteries, where the aorta connects to the RV and the pulmonary artery connects to the LV.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Failure of pulmonary veins to join the left atrium describes total anomalous pulmonary venous return, in which pulmonary veins drain into the right atrium or systemic veins instead.

questions.stem: A 2-week-old infant born to a mother with poorly controlled type 1 diabetes mellitus is brought to the pediatrician for a routine visit. The infant appears well but has mild tachypnea. A holosystolic murmur is heard at the left lower sternal border. Echocardiography confirms the suspected diagnosis. Which of the following is the most likely congenital heart defect in this infant given the maternal history and clinical findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ebstein anomaly with displacement of the tricuspid valve into the right ventricle

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pulmonary artery stenosis with right ventricular outflow tract obstruction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tetralogy of Fallot with overriding aorta and right ventricular hypertrophy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Atrial septal defect with left-to-right shunting at the level of the atria

choices.label: E
choices.text: Ventricular septal defect with left-to-right shunting at the ventricular level

explanation.summary: Maternal diabetes is a risk factor for both VSD and transposition of the great arteries. A holosystolic (regurgitant) murmur at the left lower sternal border is the classic auscultatory finding of VSD, the most common CHD overall.
explanation.whyCorrect: Maternal diabetes is specifically listed as a risk factor for VSD. The holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border is characteristic of blood flowing through the ventricular septal defect, and VSD accounts for 42% of all CHDs.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Ebstein anomaly is associated with prenatal lithium exposure, not maternal diabetes. It involves downward displacement of the tricuspid valve leaflets into the right ventricle.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pulmonary artery stenosis is associated with congenital rubella infection, not maternal diabetes. It produces a systolic ejection murmur (crescendo-decrescendo), not a holosystolic murmur.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Tetralogy of Fallot is associated with fetal alcohol syndrome and 22q11 syndrome, not maternal diabetes. It produces a systolic ejection murmur, not a holosystolic murmur.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Atrial septal defect is associated with alcohol exposure and congenital rubella, not maternal diabetes. ASD produces a midsystolic ejection murmur, not a holosystolic murmur.

questions.stem: A 3-day-old infant presents with severe cyanosis that does not improve with supplemental oxygen. Echocardiography reveals that the aorta arises from the right ventricle and the pulmonary artery arises from the left ventricle. The infant's survival currently depends on mixing of blood through a patent foramen ovale. Which of the following embryologic events failed to occur normally during cardiac development in this infant?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fusion of the endocardial cushions during atrial and ventricular septation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Spiraling of the aorticopulmonary septum during outflow tract division

choices.label: C
choices.text: Formation of the aorticopulmonary septum to divide the truncus arteriosus

choices.label: D
choices.text: Alignment of the outflow tract septum with the muscular ventricular septum

choices.label: E
choices.text: Connection of the common pulmonary vein to the posterior wall of the left atrium

explanation.summary: Transposition of the great arteries results from failure of the aorticopulmonary septum to spiral normally during development, causing the aorta to connect to the RV and the pulmonary artery to the LV.
explanation.whyCorrect: In normal development, the aorticopulmonary septum spirals as it divides the outflow tract, ensuring the aorta connects to the LV and the pulmonary artery to the RV. Failure to spiral results in transposition of the great arteries.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Failure of endocardial cushion fusion leads to atrioventricular septal defect (endocardial cushion defect), which involves abnormalities of the atrial septum, ventricular septum, and AV valves, not transposition of the great arteries.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Failure of the aorticopulmonary septum to form entirely results in persistent truncus arteriosus, a single outflow vessel, not transposition where two separate but switched vessels exist.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Misalignment of the outflow tract septum with the ventricular septum leads to tetralogy of Fallot with its four characteristic defects, not transposition of the great arteries.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Failure of the pulmonary veins to connect to the left atrium results in total anomalous pulmonary venous return, where pulmonary veins drain into the right atrium or systemic veins.

questions.stem: A 4-month-old female with Down syndrome is evaluated for failure to thrive and recurrent respiratory infections. On examination, she has a continuous machine-like murmur heard best in the left infraclavicular area. Which of the following best describes the hemodynamic consequence of this infant's cardiac defect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Deoxygenated blood flows from the right ventricle into the left ventricle through an abnormal septal opening

choices.label: B
choices.text: Deoxygenated blood from the right atrium crosses into the left atrium through a defect in the atrial septum

choices.label: C
choices.text: Blood from the left ventricle enters the right atrium through an abnormal communication between these two chambers

choices.label: D
choices.text: Oxygenated blood from the pulmonary veins drains into the right atrium instead of the left atrium

choices.label: E
choices.text: Oxygenated blood flows from the aorta into the pulmonary artery through a persistent fetal vascular connection

explanation.summary: A continuous machine-like murmur in the left infraclavicular area is the classic finding of patent ductus arteriosus. In PDA, the ductus arteriosus persists after birth, creating a left-to-right shunt from the high-pressure aorta to the lower-pressure pulmonary artery.
explanation.whyCorrect: PDA is a persistent connection between the aorta and pulmonary artery. Because aortic pressure exceeds pulmonary artery pressure, oxygenated blood flows from the aorta into the pulmonary artery (left-to-right shunt), producing increased pulmonary blood flow.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Right-to-left shunting through a VSD would cause cyanosis, and VSD produces a holosystolic murmur, not a continuous machine-like murmur. PDA involves a great vessel connection, not an interventricular communication.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Right-to-left shunting through an ASD would cause cyanosis. An ASD produces a midsystolic ejection murmur. In uncomplicated ASD, blood shunts left-to-right, not right-to-left.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A left ventricle to right atrium communication (Gerbode defect) is extremely rare and does not produce a continuous machine-like murmur characteristic of PDA.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pulmonary veins draining into the right atrium describes total anomalous pulmonary venous return, a cyanotic defect with different hemodynamics and auscultatory findings than PDA.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman with a known history of an uncorrected atrial septal defect presents with progressive dyspnea, cyanosis of the lips and nailbeds, and digital clubbing. Cardiac catheterization reveals pulmonary artery pressure of 85/40 mmHg and right-to-left shunting across the atrial septum. Which of the following clinical findings would be expected on cardiac auscultation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Continuous machine-like murmur heard best in the left infraclavicular region

choices.label: B
choices.text: Holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border radiating to the right

choices.label: C
choices.text: Diastolic rumbling murmur heard best at the cardiac apex with the patient in left lateral decubitus

choices.label: D
choices.text: Split S2 that may be appreciated on auscultation of the pulmonic area

choices.label: E
choices.text: Opening snap followed by a low-pitched diastolic murmur at the left sternal border

explanation.summary: This patient has Eisenmenger syndrome from a long-standing ASD. Pulmonary hypertension with Eisenmenger syndrome characteristically produces a split S2 on auscultation, as noted in the source material.
explanation.whyCorrect: According to the source material, pulmonary hypertension (Eisenmenger syndrome) is associated with a split S2 (ejection) on cardiac auscultation. The elevated pulmonary pressures cause delayed closure of the pulmonic valve, producing the split second heart sound.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A continuous machine-like murmur is characteristic of patent ductus arteriosus, not Eisenmenger syndrome from an ASD. Once shunt reversal occurs, the flow dynamics change and the original murmur may diminish.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border is characteristic of a ventricular septal defect, not an atrial septal defect or its complication of Eisenmenger syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A diastolic rumbling murmur at the apex is characteristic of mitral stenosis, not pulmonary hypertension from Eisenmenger syndrome secondary to an ASD.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An opening snap with a low-pitched diastolic murmur is characteristic of mitral stenosis, typically from rheumatic heart disease, and is not associated with Eisenmenger syndrome.

questions.stem: A pregnant woman undergoes a routine 20-week prenatal ultrasound. The fetal echocardiogram reveals that all four pulmonary veins drain into the right atrium rather than the left atrium. Which of the following best describes the embryologic basis of this abnormality?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Failure of the aorticopulmonary septum to spiral during division of the truncus arteriosus

choices.label: B
choices.text: Misalignment of the outflow tract septum leading to overriding of the aorta over the ventricular septum

choices.label: C
choices.text: Failure of the pulmonary veins to establish their normal connection with the left atrium during development

choices.label: D
choices.text: Incomplete closure of the foramen ovale resulting in a persistent interatrial communication after birth

choices.label: E
choices.text: Failure of the aorticopulmonary septum to form, resulting in a single undivided outflow tract

explanation.summary: Total anomalous pulmonary venous return (TAPVR) occurs when the pulmonary veins fail to join the left atrium during embryologic development, instead connecting to the right atrium or systemic veins.
explanation.whyCorrect: The embryologic basis of TAPVR is the failure of pulmonary veins to join the left atrium. This results in all pulmonary venous blood returning to the right heart, which is one of the five T cyanotic congenital heart defects.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Failure of the aorticopulmonary septum to spiral causes transposition of the great arteries, where the aorta connects to the RV and pulmonary artery to the LV, not abnormal pulmonary venous drainage.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Misalignment of the outflow tract septum is the embryologic basis for tetralogy of Fallot, which involves VSD, overriding aorta, pulmonary stenosis, and RV hypertrophy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A persistent foramen ovale is not the same as TAPVR. The foramen ovale is a normal fetal atrial communication; its failure to close results in an ASD, not anomalous pulmonary venous connections.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Failure of the aorticopulmonary septum to form results in persistent truncus arteriosus, a single outflow vessel serving both ventricles, not an abnormality of the pulmonary venous drainage.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman taking lithium for bipolar disorder becomes pregnant. She is concerned about potential effects on the developing fetus. Her psychiatrist explains that lithium use during pregnancy is specifically associated with a congenital cardiac malformation. Which of the following cardiac defects is most closely associated with this patient's medication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ventricular septal defect with left-to-right shunting and a holosystolic murmur

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tetralogy of Fallot with right ventricular outflow tract obstruction and overriding aorta

choices.label: C
choices.text: Transposition of the great arteries with the aorta arising from the right ventricle

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patent ductus arteriosus with a continuous machine-like murmur in the infraclavicular region

choices.label: E
choices.text: Ebstein anomaly with downward displacement of the tricuspid valve into the right ventricle

explanation.summary: Prenatal lithium exposure is specifically associated with Ebstein anomaly, which involves displacement of the tricuspid valve leaflets downward into the right ventricle.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ebstein anomaly is the congenital cardiac defect specifically linked to prenatal lithium exposure. It is characterized by displacement of the tricuspid valve leaflets into the right ventricle and is associated with tricuspid regurgitation, accessory conduction pathways, and right-sided heart failure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: VSD is associated with fetal alcohol exposure, maternal diabetes, and congenital rubella infection, not lithium exposure.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Tetralogy of Fallot is associated with fetal alcohol exposure and 22q11 syndrome, not prenatal lithium use.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Transposition of the great arteries is associated with maternal diabetes, not lithium exposure.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Patent ductus arteriosus is associated with fetal alcohol exposure and congenital rubella infection, not lithium use.

questions.stem: A 1-year-old child with a known large ventricular septal defect is evaluated for worsening symptoms. Physical examination reveals hepatomegaly, jugular venous distention, and lower extremity edema. Echocardiography confirms left-to-right shunting with right ventricular dilation and hypertrophy. Which of the following best explains the pathophysiologic mechanism leading to this child's hepatomegaly?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased systemic arterial oxygen delivery leading to hepatic ischemia and congestion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased pulmonary venous return to the left atrium causing elevated left atrial pressure and hepatic venous congestion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Left ventricular dilation from increased preload causing mitral regurgitation and backward transmission of pressure to the hepatic veins

choices.label: D
choices.text: Volume overload of the right ventricle causing elevated right-sided pressures with backup of blood into the systemic venous circulation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Chronic hypoxemia stimulating erythropoietin release resulting in polycythemia and hepatic sinusoidal congestion

explanation.summary: In a left-to-right VSD shunt, excess blood flows into the right ventricle and pulmonary circulation. The RV dilates and hypertrophies, leading to elevated right-sided pressures and right heart failure. This causes backup of blood into the systemic venous circulation, resulting in hepatomegaly, jugular venous distention, and peripheral edema.
explanation.whyCorrect: Left-to-right shunting causes volume overload of the right heart. The RV receives excess blood, leading to dilation, hypertrophy, and elevated right-sided pressures. Right heart failure results in backup of blood in the venous circulation, manifesting as hepatomegaly, JVD, and edema.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Left-to-right shunts are acyanotic because oxygenated blood flows into the right heart; the aortic blood remains fully oxygenated. Hepatic ischemia from decreased oxygen delivery does not explain the hepatomegaly.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While increased pulmonary blood flow increases pulmonary venous return, the hepatomegaly in this case results from right heart failure and elevated right-sided pressures, not left atrial pressure elevation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: In a VSD with left-to-right shunting, the primary volume overload affects the right ventricle, not the left ventricle. The hepatomegaly is due to right-sided heart failure, not mitral regurgitation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Left-to-right shunts do not cause chronic hypoxemia since the systemic blood remains fully oxygenated. Polycythemia-induced hepatic congestion is not the mechanism of hepatomegaly in this condition.

questions.stem: An 18-year-old female college athlete presents to the student health clinic with primary amenorrhea. She is at the 10th percentile for height and has webbing of the neck. Blood pressure is 145/92 mmHg in the right arm. A harsh systolic murmur is heard along the left sternal border and is also audible over the back. Femoral pulses are diminished bilaterally. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cardiac pathology?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bicuspid aortic valve with progressive calcification causing left ventricular outflow tract obstruction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Narrowing of the descending aorta distal to the left subclavian artery near the ligamentum arteriosum

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fibrosis and fusion of the pulmonary valve commissures causing right ventricular outflow obstruction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Membranous ventricular septal defect with left-to-right shunting and right ventricular volume overload

choices.label: E
choices.text: Atrial septal defect with progressive pulmonary hypertension and right-to-left shunt reversal

explanation.summary: This patient has Turner syndrome (45,XO) with short stature, webbed neck, and primary amenorrhea. CoA is present in 10-15% of patients with Turner syndrome. The hypertension, diminished femoral pulses, and systolic murmur radiating to the back are classic findings of coarctation of the aorta.
explanation.whyCorrect: Coarctation of the aorta is located most commonly in the descending aorta just distal to the left subclavian artery near the ligamentum arteriosum. Turner syndrome is strongly associated with CoA. The diminished femoral pulses, upper extremity hypertension, and systolic murmur heard over the back are hallmark findings.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While bicuspid aortic valve can coexist with CoA, the diminished femoral pulses and murmur radiating to the back are specific for coarctation. Aortic stenosis murmur radiates to the carotid arteries, not the back.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pulmonary valve stenosis produces a systolic ejection murmur that radiates to the back, but it would not cause upper extremity hypertension or diminished femoral pulses, which are specific to aortic obstruction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: VSD produces a holosystolic murmur at the left sternal border but does not cause upper extremity hypertension or diminished femoral pulses. VSD is not specifically associated with Turner syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.E: ASD with Eisenmenger syndrome would present with cyanosis and signs of pulmonary hypertension, not upper extremity hypertension with diminished femoral pulses.

questions.stem: A 2-day-old neonate born at term develops acute-onset pallor, tachypnea, and poor feeding. Femoral pulses are absent bilaterally. The infant rapidly deteriorates into cardiogenic shock. The pediatric cardiologist suspects a severe preductal coarctation. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial pharmacologic intervention to stabilize this neonate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Intravenous prostaglandin E1 to maintain patency of the ductus arteriosus and preserve distal perfusion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intravenous indomethacin to promote closure of the ductus arteriosus and reduce pulmonary overcirculation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Oral captopril to reduce left ventricular afterload and improve forward cardiac output through the coarctation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Intravenous furosemide to reduce intravascular volume and decrease the pressure gradient across the obstruction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Intravenous milrinone to increase left ventricular contractility and augment blood flow through the narrowed segment

explanation.summary: In neonates with severe preductal coarctation, prostaglandins are used to keep the ductus arteriosus open to maintain blood flow to the lower half of the body until corrective surgery can be performed.
explanation.whyCorrect: Prostaglandin E1 maintains ductal patency, allowing blood to bypass the coarctation through the ductus arteriosus and reach the lower body. This is a critical stabilization measure before surgical repair in severe preductal CoA.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Indomethacin promotes closure of the ductus arteriosus and would be catastrophic in this setting. The neonate depends on the PDA to maintain blood flow distal to the coarctation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: ACE inhibitors do not address the immediate life-threatening obstruction. The neonate needs ductal patency to maintain distal perfusion, not afterload reduction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Diuretics reduce intravascular volume but do not address the fundamental problem of obstructed aortic blood flow. Volume depletion could worsen shock in this setting.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While inotropes may be used as supportive therapy, they do not address the need to maintain an alternative pathway for blood flow past the coarctation. Prostaglandins to maintain ductal patency are the priority.

questions.stem: A 12-year-old boy is referred to cardiology after elevated blood pressure is discovered during a school screening. He reports occasional leg pain after running. Physical examination reveals blood pressure of 150/95 mmHg in the right arm and 120/75 mmHg in the right leg. A chest radiograph shows notching along the inferior surfaces of the 3rd through 8th ribs. Which of the following best explains the radiographic finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Erosion of the rib surfaces by enlarged intercostal arteries serving as collateral pathways around the aortic obstruction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Periosteal reaction from chronic hypoxemia causing subperiosteal new bone formation along the rib margins

choices.label: C
choices.text: Osteoporotic changes in the ribs due to secondary hyperparathyroidism from chronic renal hypoperfusion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Compression of the ribs by dilated internal thoracic veins draining increased blood volume from the upper body

choices.label: E
choices.text: Calcification of the intercostal arteries from chronic hypertension causing visible radiodensities along the rib margins

explanation.summary: Rib notching on chest X-ray in coarctation of the aorta is caused by intercostal arteries that enlarge to provide collateral circulation and erode the lower surfaces of the ribs, typically from the 3rd to the 8th ribs.
explanation.whyCorrect: In CoA, the body develops collateral circulation to bypass the aortic narrowing. The intercostal arteries enlarge as collateral vessels, and their progressive enlargement erodes the inferior surfaces of the ribs, producing the characteristic notching seen on chest X-ray.

whyWrongByChoice.B: CoA is an acyanotic condition that does not cause chronic hypoxemia. Rib notching is not related to periosteal reaction from hypoxemia but rather to mechanical erosion by enlarged collateral arteries.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While the lower body may have reduced perfusion, rib notching is caused by arterial erosion, not osteoporotic changes. Secondary hyperparathyroidism is not a recognized complication of CoA.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The collateral circulation involves arteries, not veins. The intercostal arteries enlarge to carry blood around the coarctation, and their pulsatile flow erodes the ribs from below.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The rib notching is caused by physical erosion from enlarged tortuous intercostal arteries, not by calcification of the arteries. The notching represents bone loss, not calcium deposition.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man presents with new-onset headaches, visual disturbances, and a blood pressure of 180/110 mmHg. He has no significant past medical history. Physical examination reveals diminished femoral pulses and a blood pressure of 125/80 mmHg in the lower extremities. Echocardiography confirms the suspected diagnosis. Which of the following complications is this patient most at risk for if the condition remains untreated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Right ventricular hypertrophy from chronic pressure overload of the pulmonary circulation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Infective endocarditis from turbulent flow across the mitral valve causing valvular damage

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cerebral aneurysm from sustained secondary hypertension proximal to the obstruction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pulmonary hypertension from chronic volume overload of the right heart chambers

choices.label: E
choices.text: Aortic dissection from chronic hypotension in the descending thoracic aorta distal to the lesion

explanation.summary: Untreated coarctation of the aorta causes secondary hypertension proximal to the narrowing, which can lead to complications including left ventricular hypertrophy, atherosclerosis, and cerebral aneurysms.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cerebral aneurysms are a recognized complication of untreated CoA. The sustained hypertension proximal to the coarctation affects the cerebral vasculature, predisposing to aneurysm formation. This, along with LVH and atherosclerosis, are the key complications listed for untreated CoA.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CoA is a left-sided obstructive lesion that primarily affects the left ventricle through increased afterload. Right ventricular hypertrophy is not a direct complication of coarctation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While turbulent flow is present at the coarctation site, infective endocarditis of the mitral valve is not a listed complication of CoA. The primary complications are LVH, atherosclerosis, and cerebral aneurysms.
whyWrongByChoice.D: CoA does not cause chronic volume overload of the right heart. Pulmonary hypertension is a complication of left-to-right shunts, not of aortic obstruction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The descending aorta distal to the CoA has reduced blood pressure, not hypotension sufficient to cause dissection. Aortic dissection distal to the lesion is not a recognized complication of CoA.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old boy is referred for evaluation of an incidentally discovered heart murmur. He is asymptomatic and has normal growth and development. Cardiac auscultation reveals a harsh systolic ejection murmur best heard at the left upper sternal border that radiates to the back. ECG shows right-axis deviation. Echocardiography demonstrates a thickened pulmonary valve with fused commissures. Which of the following pathologic findings would be expected in the affected valve?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Myxomatous degeneration with redundant leaflet tissue and chordal elongation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Calcific nodules on the ventricular surface of the valve from chronic rheumatic inflammation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Vegetations composed of fibrin and platelets adherent to the valve surface from endocarditis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fibrosis causing valve thickening with fusion of the commissures narrowing the outflow tract

choices.label: E
choices.text: Carcinoid plaques causing endocardial thickening and retraction of the valve leaflets

explanation.summary: In congenital pulmonary valve stenosis, fibrosis causes the pulmonary valve to thicken and the commissures to fuse, narrowing the outflow tract and reducing blood flow to the lungs.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material specifically states that in congenital pulmonary valve stenosis, fibrosis causes the pulmonary valve to thicken and the commissures to fuse. This is the characteristic pathologic finding of congenital PVS.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Myxomatous degeneration with redundant leaflets and chordal elongation is characteristic of mitral valve prolapse, not congenital pulmonary valve stenosis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Calcific nodules from rheumatic inflammation are characteristic of rheumatic valve disease, which most commonly affects the mitral valve and is an acquired condition, not a congenital one.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Fibrin-platelet vegetations are characteristic of infective endocarditis, an acquired infectious condition, not the underlying pathology of congenital pulmonary valve stenosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Carcinoid plaques causing valve thickening and retraction are seen in carcinoid heart disease, which typically affects the right-sided valves but is an acquired condition from serotonin-secreting tumors.

questions.stem: A neonate with severe pulmonary atresia is diagnosed shortly after birth. The infant is cyanotic and tachypneic. Echocardiography reveals no communication between the right ventricle and the pulmonary artery, a hypoplastic right ventricle, and an atrial septal defect. Which of the following additional findings is necessary to maintain pulmonary blood flow in this infant?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Large ventricular septal defect allowing blood to flow from the left ventricle to the right ventricle and into the pulmonary artery

choices.label: B
choices.text: Collateral bronchial arteries arising from the descending aorta supplying the pulmonary vascular bed

choices.label: C
choices.text: Persistent left superior vena cava draining directly into the left pulmonary artery for partial lung perfusion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Anomalous origin of the right coronary artery from the pulmonary trunk providing retrograde pulmonary flow

choices.label: E
choices.text: Patent ductus arteriosus providing a connection between the aorta and the pulmonary artery for lung perfusion

explanation.summary: When the pulmonary valve is entirely atretic (pulmonary atresia), there is no communication between the right ventricle and the lungs. The anomaly is associated with a hypoplastic RV and an ASD, and blood reaches the lungs through a patent ductus arteriosus.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material specifically states that in pulmonary atresia, the anomaly is associated with a hypoplastic right ventricle and an ASD, and blood reaches the lungs through a patent ductus arteriosus. The PDA is essential for maintaining pulmonary blood flow.

whyWrongByChoice.A: With pulmonary atresia and a hypoplastic RV, blood cannot pass through the right ventricle to the pulmonary artery even with a VSD. The PDA provides the necessary aorta-to-pulmonary artery connection.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Bronchial collateral arteries are not the primary mechanism for maintaining pulmonary blood flow in neonatal pulmonary atresia. The PDA is the recognized pathway described for this condition.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A persistent left superior vena cava draining into the pulmonary artery is not a recognized compensatory mechanism in pulmonary atresia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Anomalous coronary artery origin from the pulmonary trunk is a separate congenital anomaly and does not provide meaningful retrograde pulmonary blood flow in pulmonary atresia.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with exertional dyspnea, dizziness, and chest pain that have been worsening over the past year. He was told he had a heart murmur as a child but was never followed up. On examination, a crescendo-decrescendo systolic ejection murmur is heard best at the right upper sternal border with radiation to the carotid arteries. Echocardiography reveals a bicuspid aortic valve with significant stenosis. Which of the following best explains why this patient's symptoms are presenting now rather than in childhood?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The bicuspid valve predisposes to progressive fibrosis and calcification, causing worsening stenosis with age

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rheumatic fever likely occurred in adolescence and caused additional inflammatory damage to the valve

choices.label: C
choices.text: The aortic root has dilated with age, causing relative stenosis at the valve level despite unchanged leaflet pathology

choices.label: D
choices.text: Age-related myocardial fibrosis has reduced left ventricular compliance, unmasking previously compensated stenosis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Chronic systemic hypertension has increased left ventricular afterload, making the fixed obstruction clinically significant

explanation.summary: Congenital AVS with a bicuspid valve is a progressive disease. Having two cusps rather than the normal three predisposes the valve to fibrosis and calcification with age, causing worsening stenosis over time.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material states that having two cusps rather than the normal three predisposes the valve to fibrosis and calcification (and worsening stenosis) with age. This progressive nature explains why a patient with mild childhood disease can develop symptomatic stenosis in adulthood.

whyWrongByChoice.B: There is no indication of rheumatic fever in this patient's history. The progressive stenosis of a bicuspid aortic valve is due to inherent structural predisposition to fibrosis and calcification, not superimposed rheumatic disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Aortic root dilation does not cause relative valve stenosis. The stenosis in bicuspid aortic valve disease results from progressive fibrosis and calcification of the valve leaflets themselves.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While age-related changes can affect myocardial function, the primary reason for symptom progression in congenital AVS is the progressive fibrosis and calcification of the bicuspid valve, not myocardial fibrosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The stenosis is due to progressive valve pathology, not increased afterload from hypertension. The bicuspid valve itself undergoes fibrosis and calcification that worsens the obstruction over time.

questions.stem: A 7-year-old girl with no prior medical history is evaluated for claudication and chest pain during physical activity. Blood pressure in the right arm is 155/95 mmHg. Echocardiography with Doppler reveals a discrete narrowing of the descending thoracic aorta. Which of the following best classifies this patient's hypertension?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Essential hypertension from a genetic predisposition to elevated systemic vascular resistance

choices.label: B
choices.text: Renovascular hypertension from renal artery stenosis caused by fibromuscular dysplasia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Secondary hypertension from increased arterial pressure proximal to the aortic obstruction

choices.label: D
choices.text: White coat hypertension from anxiety-related sympathetic activation during the clinical encounter

choices.label: E
choices.text: Endocrine hypertension from excess catecholamine secretion by a pheochromocytoma

explanation.summary: Coarctation of the aorta is a leading cause of secondary hypertension in children. The aortic narrowing increases resistance to blood flow, causing elevated arterial pressure proximal to the stenosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material identifies CoA as a leading cause of secondary hypertension in children. The body compensates for the obstruction by increasing systemic blood pressure, resulting in higher arterial pressures proximal to the coarctation (in the upper extremities and cranium).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Essential hypertension is a diagnosis of exclusion and is rare in children. This patient has a clear secondary cause of hypertension: the aortic obstruction creating increased resistance to blood flow.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Renovascular hypertension from fibromuscular dysplasia is a different cause of secondary hypertension. This patient's hypertension is directly caused by the aortic obstruction identified on echocardiography.
whyWrongByChoice.D: White coat hypertension does not explain the claudication, chest pain with exertion, and echocardiographic findings of aortic narrowing in this patient.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pheochromocytoma causes episodic hypertension from catecholamine excess. This patient's hypertension has a clear structural cause demonstrated on echocardiography.

questions.stem: A newborn is diagnosed with critical aortic valve stenosis on fetal echocardiography. Within the first few days of life, the infant develops signs of cardiogenic shock as the ductus arteriosus begins to close. The pediatric cardiologist explains that this severe form of aortic stenosis can lead to underdevelopment of the left ventricle and ascending aorta. Which of the following terms best describes this constellation of findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tetralogy of Fallot with severe right ventricular outflow tract obstruction and overriding aorta

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hypoplastic left heart syndrome with underdeveloped left ventricle and ascending aorta

choices.label: C
choices.text: Persistent truncus arteriosus with a single outflow tract supplying systemic and pulmonary circulations

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ebstein anomaly with apical displacement of the tricuspid valve and atrialized right ventricle

choices.label: E
choices.text: Coarctation of the aorta with secondary left ventricular hypertrophy and collateral vessel formation

explanation.summary: In severe congenital aortic stenosis or atresia, obstruction of the left ventricular outflow tract leads to underdevelopment (hypoplasia) of the left ventricle and ascending aorta. This constellation of findings is called hypoplastic left heart syndrome.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material specifically states that in severe congenital aortic stenosis or atresia, obstruction of the left ventricular outflow tract leads to hypoplasia of the left ventricle and ascending aorta, and this is called hypoplastic left heart syndrome. It is nearly always fatal in the first week when the ductus closes unless intervention is performed.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Tetralogy of Fallot involves right ventricular outflow obstruction, not left ventricular outflow obstruction. It is a cyanotic defect with different anatomy than hypoplastic left heart syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Persistent truncus arteriosus involves failure of the aorticopulmonary septum to form, resulting in a single outflow vessel. It does not involve left ventricular hypoplasia from aortic valve stenosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ebstein anomaly involves the tricuspid valve and right ventricle, not the aortic valve or left ventricle. It is associated with lithium exposure, not aortic outflow obstruction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Coarctation of the aorta involves narrowing of the descending aorta, not the aortic valve or left ventricular outflow tract. CoA causes LV hypertrophy, not LV hypoplasia.

questions.stem: A 16-year-old male presents for a sports physical. He reports no symptoms. Blood pressure is 148/90 mmHg in both arms. Physical examination reveals a systolic murmur heard over the left chest as well as flow murmurs over the back and lateral chest wall. Echocardiography reveals a ductal coarctation. Which of the following additional cardiac malformations is most commonly associated with this patient's condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mitral valve prolapse with myxomatous degeneration of the posterior mitral leaflet

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tricuspid atresia with absent communication between the right atrium and right ventricle

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bicuspid aortic valve with two cusps instead of the normal three cusps

choices.label: D
choices.text: Total anomalous pulmonary venous return with pulmonary veins draining into the right atrium

choices.label: E
choices.text: Transposition of the great arteries with the aorta arising from the right ventricle

explanation.summary: Coarctation of the aorta is associated with several other CHDs including bicuspid aortic valve, ventricular septal defect, and patent ductus arteriosus. Bicuspid aortic valve is the most commonly associated malformation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material specifically lists bicuspid aortic valve as one of the associated CHDs with coarctation of the aorta. Both conditions share developmental origins related to abnormal formation of left-sided cardiac structures.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Mitral valve prolapse is not listed as an associated defect with coarctation of the aorta. The commonly associated defects are bicuspid aortic valve, VSD, and PDA.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Tricuspid atresia is a cyanotic right-to-left shunt defect and is not specifically associated with coarctation of the aorta.
whyWrongByChoice.D: TAPVR is a cyanotic defect involving anomalous pulmonary venous drainage and is not associated with coarctation of the aorta.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Transposition of the great arteries is associated with maternal diabetes and involves failure of the aorticopulmonary septum to spiral. It is not specifically associated with CoA.

questions.stem: A 14-year-old boy with moderate pulmonary valve stenosis is being followed with serial echocardiograms. Over the past two years, echocardiography has shown progressive thickening of the right ventricular free wall. He now reports mild exertional dyspnea. ECG demonstrates right-axis deviation and right ventricular hypertrophy. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of right ventricular wall thickening in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chronic volume overload from a coexisting left-to-right shunt through an undiagnosed atrial septal defect

choices.label: B
choices.text: Myocardial infiltration by amyloid protein causing concentric increase in right ventricular wall thickness

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chronic pulmonary thromboembolic disease causing progressive right ventricular pressure overload

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased right ventricular afterload from the stenotic pulmonary valve causing compensatory myocardial hypertrophy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased preload from elevated systemic venous return causing eccentric right ventricular remodeling

explanation.summary: In pulmonary valve stenosis, the obstructed outflow tract increases right ventricular afterload. The RV compensates with myocardial hypertrophy to maintain cardiac output against the increased resistance, which is the mechanism leading to RVH and eventually heart failure.
explanation.whyCorrect: The stenotic pulmonary valve creates increased resistance to right ventricular outflow (increased afterload). The RV adapts by developing wall hypertrophy to generate higher pressures. Long-standing disease leads to progressive RVH and eventually heart failure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: There is no evidence of an ASD in this patient. The RVH is caused by pressure overload from the pulmonary valve stenosis, not volume overload from a shunt.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Amyloid infiltration causes restrictive cardiomyopathy and is not related to congenital pulmonary valve stenosis. The wall thickening in this case is from myocardial hypertrophy, not infiltrative disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension is an acquired condition unrelated to congenital pulmonary valve stenosis. The RVH here is directly caused by the valvular obstruction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The RVH in pulmonary valve stenosis is caused by pressure overload (increased afterload), not volume overload. Eccentric remodeling occurs with volume overload, while concentric hypertrophy occurs with pressure overload.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with a history of coarctation of the aorta repaired in childhood presents with recurrent hypertension. She underwent surgical resection with end-to-end anastomosis at age 3. Her blood pressure is 160/100 mmHg in both arms and 145/90 mmHg in the legs. She is referred for imaging to evaluate for recoarctation. Which of the following imaging modalities is the first-line diagnostic study to assess this patient's aortic pathology?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chest radiography to evaluate for rib notching and mediastinal contour abnormalities

choices.label: B
choices.text: Echocardiography with Doppler to detect the aortic narrowing and estimate the severity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cardiac catheterization with angiography to directly measure the transcoarctation pressure gradient

choices.label: D
choices.text: Electrocardiography to assess for left ventricular hypertrophy and strain pattern

choices.label: E
choices.text: Nuclear perfusion imaging to evaluate myocardial ischemia from chronic pressure overload

explanation.summary: Echocardiography with Doppler is the imaging modality of choice for diagnosing coarctation of the aorta, as it can detect the aortic narrowing and estimate the severity of obstruction.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material explicitly states that echocardiography with Doppler is the imaging modality of choice for CoA, as it can be used to detect the aortic narrowing and estimate the severity. This applies to both initial diagnosis and evaluation of recoarctation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Chest radiography is not required for diagnosis of CoA, though it may show rib notching in uncorrected cases. It is less sensitive and specific than echocardiography for assessing recoarctation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cardiac catheterization is an invasive procedure used for intervention, not as a first-line diagnostic study. Echocardiography provides noninvasive assessment of the narrowing and its hemodynamic significance.
whyWrongByChoice.D: ECG may show LVH but cannot directly visualize the aortic narrowing or assess its severity. It provides indirect evidence only and is not the first-line imaging study for CoA.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Nuclear perfusion imaging assesses myocardial perfusion and is not used to diagnose or assess coarctation of the aorta.

questions.stem: A 3-week-old female infant born at 28 weeks gestation is evaluated in the neonatal intensive care unit for persistent tachypnea and difficulty feeding. She had respiratory distress syndrome at birth requiring surfactant therapy. On examination, the infant is afebrile with a heart rate of 160/min and respiratory rate of 68/min. A continuous machine-like murmur is best heard over the left subclavicular region. Bounding peripheral pulses and a widened pulse pressure are noted. Echocardiography with Doppler confirms the suspected diagnosis. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial pharmacologic management for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prostaglandin E1 infusion to maintain patency of the ductus arteriosus

choices.label: B
choices.text: Furosemide to reduce pulmonary edema and decrease volume overload

choices.label: C
choices.text: Indomethacin to inhibit prostaglandin synthesis and promote ductal closure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Propranolol to reduce heart rate and decrease myocardial oxygen demand

choices.label: E
choices.text: Digoxin to improve cardiac contractility and increase cardiac output

explanation.summary: Premature infants with patent ductus arteriosus (PDA) are initially managed with cyclooxygenase (COX) inhibitors such as indomethacin or ibuprofen, which induce closure by blocking PGE1 production.
explanation.whyCorrect: COX inhibitors like indomethacin block prostaglandin E1 production, which is the mediator keeping the ductus arteriosus patent. This is the first-line pharmacologic treatment for PDA in premature infants, as declining PGE1 levels are closely linked to DA closure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Prostaglandin E1 infusion would keep the ductus arteriosus open, which is the opposite of the treatment goal in PDA. PGE1 is used in cyanotic CHDs where ductal patency is needed for survival.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While furosemide may be used as adjunctive therapy for heart failure symptoms, it does not address the underlying pathology of a patent ductus arteriosus and is not the initial treatment of choice.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Propranolol is not used in PDA management. It is used in tetralogy of Fallot to relax RVOT obstruction during tet spells, not for ductal closure.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Digoxin may be used for heart failure management but does not promote closure of the ductus arteriosus, which is the primary therapeutic goal in this premature infant.

questions.stem: A 7-year-old boy presents with progressive exercise intolerance and fatigue over the past year. His mother notes that he frequently squats during play. On examination, cyanosis and clubbing are observed in the toes but not the fingers. Pulse oximetry shows oxygen saturation of 98% in the right upper extremity and 82% in the right lower extremity. A loud systolic murmur is heard. Which of the following best explains the pattern of cyanosis observed in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Deoxygenated blood enters the aorta proximal to the left subclavian artery origin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Deoxygenated blood enters the aorta distal to the arteries supplying the upper extremities

choices.label: C
choices.text: Oxygenated blood is preferentially shunted to the lower extremity vasculature

choices.label: D
choices.text: Thromboemboli from the right atrium preferentially travel to the lower extremities

choices.label: E
choices.text: Peripheral vascular disease in the lower extremities reduces oxygen delivery distally

explanation.summary: Differential cyanosis with blue lower extremities and pink upper extremities occurs in Eisenmenger syndrome complicating PDA, where deoxygenated blood enters the descending aorta distal to the upper extremity arterial branches.
explanation.whyCorrect: In PDA with Eisenmenger syndrome, the shunt reverses to right-to-left, causing deoxygenated blood to flow from the pulmonary artery into the descending aorta distal to where the left subclavian artery exits. Thus, the upper extremities receive normally oxygenated blood while the lower extremities receive deoxygenated blood.

whyWrongByChoice.A: If deoxygenated blood entered proximal to the left subclavian artery, the upper extremities would also be cyanotic. The hallmark of PDA-related Eisenmenger syndrome is that deoxygenated blood enters distal to the upper extremity branches.
whyWrongByChoice.C: There is no preferential shunting of oxygenated blood to the lower extremities. The differential cyanosis results from the anatomic location where deoxygenated blood enters the aorta, not from selective distribution of oxygenated blood.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Thromboemboli do not explain differential cyanosis. The pattern is due to the anatomic relationship between the PDA insertion point and the upper extremity arterial branches.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Peripheral vascular disease would not produce the characteristic differential oxygen saturation pattern seen here and is exceedingly rare in a 7-year-old child.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old girl is referred for evaluation of a heart murmur detected during a routine physical examination. She is asymptomatic and has normal growth parameters. On cardiac auscultation, a systolic murmur is heard best at the second left intercostal space, and there is a wide, fixed splitting of S2 that does not vary with respiration. An echocardiogram with Doppler reveals an opening in the central portion of the interatrial septum near the fossa ovalis. Which of the following embryologic mechanisms most likely accounts for this defect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Failure of the septum primum to fuse with the endocardial cushions

choices.label: B
choices.text: Abnormal entry of the superior vena cava straddling the atrial septum

choices.label: C
choices.text: Failure of the foramen ovale flap to close after birth due to low left atrial pressure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Excessive resorption of the septum primum or inadequate growth of septum secundum

choices.label: E
choices.text: Abnormal tissue separation between the coronary sinus and the left atrium

explanation.summary: An ostium secundum ASD, located near the fossa ovalis in the central atrial septum, results from excessive resorption of the septum primum or inadequate growth of the septum secundum. It is the most common type, accounting for 70% of ASDs.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ostium secundum defects occur near the fossa ovalis due to excessive resorption of septum primum or inadequate development of septum secundum. This leaves a persistent communication in the central portion of the atrial septum.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Failure of septum primum to fuse with endocardial cushions results in an ostium primum defect, which is located in the lower (ventral) portion of the atrial septum, not near the fossa ovalis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Abnormal SVC entry straddling the atrial septum describes a sinus venosus defect, which accounts for only 5-10% of ASDs and is not located near the fossa ovalis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Failure of foramen ovale closure describes a patent foramen ovale (PFO), which is not considered a true ASD because no atrial septal tissue is missing. PFOs occur in up to 25% of the population.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Abnormalities in tissue separating the coronary sinus and left atrium cause coronary sinus defects, which account for less than 1% of ASDs.

questions.stem: A 2-month-old male infant is brought to the pediatrician for poor weight gain and rapid breathing during feeds. He was born at term without complications. Physical examination reveals tachypnea, subcostal retractions, and hepatomegaly. Cardiac auscultation reveals a holosystolic murmur best heard at the left lower sternal border with a palpable thrill. No cyanosis is observed. Which of the following hemodynamic changes is most likely present on cardiac catheterization?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased oxygen saturation in the right ventricle compared to the right atrium

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased oxygen saturation in the left atrium compared to the right atrium

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased oxygen saturation in the pulmonary artery compared to the aorta

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased oxygen saturation in the right ventricle compared to the inferior vena cava

choices.label: E
choices.text: Equal oxygen saturation in all four cardiac chambers and the great vessels

explanation.summary: In a VSD with left-to-right shunting, oxygenated blood from the LV crosses into the RV, causing a step-up in oxygen saturation in the RV compared to the RA.
explanation.whyCorrect: The left-to-right shunt across the VSD introduces oxygenated blood from the LV into the RV. Cardiac catheterization demonstrates increased oxygen saturation in the RV (and pulmonary artery) compared to the RA, known as an oxygen step-up at the ventricular level.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The left atrium receives oxygenated blood from the pulmonary veins and would not have decreased oxygen saturation compared to the RA. Left-to-right shunting does not decrease left atrial oxygen content.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While the pulmonary artery oxygen saturation is elevated in VSD due to left-to-right shunting, this answer compares it to the aorta, which normally has the highest oxygen saturation. The characteristic finding is the step-up from RA to RV.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The RV oxygen saturation would be increased, not decreased, compared to the IVC due to the left-to-right shunt delivering oxygenated blood into the RV.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Equal saturations in all chambers would suggest no shunting. In a VSD, there is a clear oxygen step-up at the RV level due to left-to-right shunting.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant with a known large ventricular septal defect is followed by a pediatric cardiologist. Over several clinic visits, the cardiologist notes that the previously loud holosystolic murmur has become progressively softer. The infant has also developed increasing tachypnea, hepatomegaly, and new-onset mild cyanosis on room air. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for the decreasing murmur intensity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Spontaneous closure of the ventricular septal defect over time

choices.label: B
choices.text: Development of infective endocarditis causing valvular obstruction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Progressive increase in right ventricular pressure reducing the shunt gradient

choices.label: D
choices.text: Compensatory left ventricular hypertrophy increasing systemic cardiac output

choices.label: E
choices.text: Development of aortic regurgitation redirecting blood flow away from the defect

explanation.summary: A progressively softer VSD murmur with worsening cyanosis suggests rising RV pressures from pulmonary vascular remodeling (early Eisenmenger syndrome), which reduces the LV-to-RV pressure gradient and thus the shunt volume and murmur intensity.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ongoing left-to-right shunting causes remodeling of the pulmonary vasculature, leading to elevated pulmonary arterial and RV pressures. As RV pressure rises, the pressure gradient between the LV and RV decreases, reducing left-to-right shunting and making the murmur softer. The new cyanosis indicates early reversal of the shunt.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Spontaneous closure would decrease the murmur but would also improve symptoms, not cause worsening tachypnea, hepatomegaly, and new cyanosis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Infective endocarditis would present with fever, new or changing murmurs, and systemic signs of infection, not the progressive hemodynamic changes described.
whyWrongByChoice.D: LV hypertrophy is a compensatory mechanism but would not explain a softer murmur or the development of cyanosis. A stronger LV would if anything maintain or increase the shunt.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aortic regurgitation is not a typical complication of VSD and would not explain the progressive softening of the murmur combined with cyanosis.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old girl presents for evaluation of a heart murmur first noted at her well-child visit. She has been asymptomatic with normal growth and development. On examination, a wide, fixed split S2 is noted. No murmur is directly attributable to the defect itself. However, a systolic murmur is heard at the second left intercostal space that increases with inspiration. Which of the following best explains why the defect itself does not produce a murmur?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The defect is too small to allow any significant blood flow across the septum

choices.label: B
choices.text: The blood flow across the defect occurs only during diastole when the heart is quiet

choices.label: C
choices.text: The defect is located in the muscular portion of the septum which dampens sound

choices.label: D
choices.text: The blood crossing the defect flows in a retrograde direction that cancels out sound waves

choices.label: E
choices.text: The pressure difference between the two chambers is insufficient to create turbulent flow

explanation.summary: In ASD, the pressure difference between the left and right atria is not significant enough to produce turbulent blood flow, so the defect itself does not generate a murmur.
explanation.whyCorrect: The ASD itself does not produce a murmur because the pressure gradient between the LA and RA is relatively small. Turbulent flow requires a significant pressure difference, and the interatrial pressure difference in ASD is insufficient. The systolic murmur heard is a flow murmur from increased volume crossing the pulmonic valve.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The question states the patient has a wide, fixed split S2, indicating a hemodynamically significant ASD with meaningful shunting. The defect is not too small; rather, the pressure gradient is too low for turbulence.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Blood flow across the ASD occurs throughout the cardiac cycle, not exclusively during diastole. The absence of a murmur is due to low-pressure gradients, not timing of flow.
whyWrongByChoice.C: ASDs occur in the atrial septum, not the muscular ventricular septum. Additionally, the muscular composition of surrounding tissue does not dampen murmur production.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sound wave cancellation is not a physiologic mechanism for murmur absence. The lack of turbulence due to low pressure gradients is the correct explanation.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and chronic kidney disease presents with progressive dyspnea on exertion, fatigue, and lower extremity edema over the past 6 months. Cardiac examination reveals a wide, fixed split S2 and a systolic flow murmur at the pulmonic area. Echocardiography reveals an atrial septal defect with evidence of pulmonary hypertension. Compared to an otherwise healthy patient of the same age with a similar-sized ASD, this patient's earlier onset of pulmonary hypertension is best explained by which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased cardiac output from chronic anemia associated with kidney disease

choices.label: B
choices.text: Poorly compliant left ventricle increasing left-to-right shunting across the defect

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chronic hypervolemia from renal sodium retention causing direct pulmonary edema

choices.label: D
choices.text: Diabetic cardiomyopathy causing primary right ventricular failure and congestion

choices.label: E
choices.text: Accelerated atherosclerosis of the pulmonary arteries from chronic hyperglycemia

explanation.summary: Patients with poorly compliant left ventricles, such as those with diabetes or chronic kidney disease, are at highest risk for developing pulmonary hypertension earlier in life when they have an ASD.
explanation.whyCorrect: A poorly compliant (stiff) LV increases left atrial pressure, which in turn increases left-to-right shunting across the ASD. This increased shunt volume leads to greater pulmonary blood flow and earlier development of pulmonary hypertension.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While anemia can increase cardiac output, the source material specifically identifies poor LV compliance (from conditions like diabetes and CKD) as the risk factor for earlier pulmonary hypertension in ASD patients.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While hypervolemia can worsen heart failure, the earlier onset of pulmonary hypertension in this context is specifically attributed to LV noncompliance increasing the shunt fraction, not direct fluid overload.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The pathophysiology involves increased left-to-right shunting from a noncompliant LV leading to pulmonary hypertension, not primary right ventricular failure from cardiomyopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Accelerated atherosclerosis of pulmonary arteries from hyperglycemia is not a recognized mechanism. The earlier pulmonary hypertension is due to increased shunting from poor LV compliance.

questions.stem: A newborn male infant delivered at 41 weeks gestation is evaluated on the second day of life. Pregnancy was complicated by first-trimester maternal rubella infection. The infant is feeding poorly and appears tachypneic. On auscultation, a continuous murmur is heard. Echocardiography confirms a large patent ductus arteriosus. The pediatrician initiates treatment with ibuprofen, but after two courses the PDA remains open. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Administer a third course of ibuprofen at a higher dose to achieve ductal closure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Begin prostaglandin E1 infusion to maintain systemic circulation through the ductus

choices.label: C
choices.text: Initiate diuretic therapy and observe for spontaneous closure over the next month

choices.label: D
choices.text: Refer for percutaneous catheter-based occlusion or surgical ligation of the PDA

choices.label: E
choices.text: Start oral sildenafil to reduce pulmonary hypertension and decrease shunt volume

explanation.summary: COX inhibitors are generally not effective for closing PDAs in term infants. Premature infants who fail medical therapy and symptomatic term infants are referred for percutaneous catheter procedures or surgical ligation.
explanation.whyCorrect: COX inhibitors are generally not effective in closing PDAs in term infants. After failing medical therapy, the next step is referral for corrective intervention, with percutaneous catheter-based occlusion being the preferred procedure. Surgical ligation is another option if percutaneous procedures are unavailable.

whyWrongByChoice.A: COX inhibitors are generally not effective for PDA closure in term infants. Repeating at higher doses is unlikely to be successful and risks adverse effects.
whyWrongByChoice.B: PGE1 infusion keeps the ductus arteriosus open and is used in cyanotic CHDs where ductal patency is needed for survival. It would worsen this patient's condition.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While some PDAs close spontaneously in the first few months, this infant is already symptomatic with a large PDA. Observation alone is inappropriate for a symptomatic large defect that has failed medical therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Sildenafil is not the standard management for PDA. The definitive treatment for a symptomatic PDA that fails medical therapy is procedural closure.

questions.stem: A 10-month-old infant with a moderate ventricular septal defect is brought to the pediatric cardiologist for follow-up. The parents report no feeding difficulties, and the child is gaining weight appropriately. Physical examination reveals a grade 4/6 holosystolic murmur with a thrill at the left sternal border. Which of the following best explains the presence of a louder murmur and palpable thrill despite a smaller defect size?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Smaller defects are associated with higher pulmonary vascular resistance

choices.label: B
choices.text: Smaller defects have increased right ventricular compliance allowing more flow

choices.label: C
choices.text: Smaller defects cause greater left ventricular hypertrophy amplifying the murmur

choices.label: D
choices.text: Smaller defects are associated with concurrent mitral valve prolapse producing sound

choices.label: E
choices.text: Smaller defects generate greater turbulence across the ventricular septum

explanation.summary: In VSD, smaller defects tend to produce louder, higher-pitched murmurs because the turbulent flow across the narrow defect is greater. The turbulence can be so strong that a palpable thrill is appreciated.
explanation.whyCorrect: Smaller VSDs create greater turbulence because the high-pressure gradient between the LV and RV forces blood through a narrow opening, producing more turbulent flow. This generates louder, higher-pitched murmurs and sometimes a palpable thrill over the left sternal border.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pulmonary vascular resistance is not directly related to defect size in this context. Higher PVR would actually reduce the shunt gradient and decrease murmur intensity.
whyWrongByChoice.B: RV compliance does not increase with smaller defects, and this is not the mechanism for louder murmurs. The loudness is due to turbulence from the narrow opening.
whyWrongByChoice.C: LV hypertrophy is more associated with larger defects causing greater volume overload. It does not amplify the murmur directly.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Concurrent mitral valve prolapse is not a characteristic association with smaller VSDs and does not explain the holosystolic murmur with thrill at the left sternal border.

questions.stem: A pediatric cardiology fellow is reviewing cardiac catheterization data from a 3-year-old patient suspected of having a congenital heart defect. The data shows the following oxygen saturations: superior vena cava 70%, inferior vena cava 72%, right atrium 85%, right ventricle 86%, pulmonary artery 86%, left atrium 98%, left ventricle 98%, aorta 97%. At which level does the oxygen step-up first occur, and which defect does this most likely represent?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ventricular level step-up consistent with a ventricular septal defect

choices.label: B
choices.text: Atrial level step-up consistent with an atrial septal defect

choices.label: C
choices.text: Great vessel level step-up consistent with a patent ductus arteriosus

choices.label: D
choices.text: Atrial level step-up consistent with total anomalous pulmonary venous return

choices.label: E
choices.text: Ventricular level step-up consistent with an atrioventricular canal defect

explanation.summary: The oxygen step-up occurs at the atrial level (SVC/IVC ~71% vs RA 85%), indicating left-to-right shunting at the atrial level, consistent with an atrial septal defect.
explanation.whyCorrect: The step-up in oxygen saturation from the SVC/IVC (70-72%) to the RA (85%) indicates that oxygenated blood is being shunted into the RA from the LA through an atrial septal defect. In ASD, blood samples from the RA, RV, and pulmonary artery show higher oxygen content than those in the SVC and IVC.

whyWrongByChoice.A: In a VSD, the step-up would occur at the ventricular level (RA oxygen saturation would be similar to SVC/IVC, with the step-up occurring from RA to RV). Here the step-up is at the atrial level.
whyWrongByChoice.C: In PDA, the step-up occurs at the great vessel level, with the pulmonary artery having higher oxygen saturation than the RV. Here the step-up is at the atrial level.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While TAPVR can cause an atrial level step-up, it is a cyanotic CHD that would present with decreased left-sided saturations. The normal left heart saturations (98%) make ASD more likely.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An AV canal defect could show step-ups at multiple levels, but the isolated atrial step-up with normal ventricular values is most consistent with a simple ASD.

questions.stem: A first-year medical student is studying cardiac embryology. She learns that the ductus arteriosus is formed from a specific embryologic structure and connects two major vessels during fetal development. Which of the following correctly describes the embryologic origin and connections of the ductus arteriosus?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Derived from the left 6th aortic arch, connecting the pulmonary arteries to the descending aorta

choices.label: B
choices.text: Derived from the left 4th aortic arch, connecting the ascending aorta to the pulmonary veins

choices.label: C
choices.text: Derived from the right 6th aortic arch, connecting the pulmonary arteries to the ascending aorta

choices.label: D
choices.text: Derived from the left 3rd aortic arch, connecting the right ventricle to the descending aorta

choices.label: E
choices.text: Derived from the truncus arteriosus, connecting the single outflow tract to the pulmonary veins

explanation.summary: The ductus arteriosus is formed from the left 6th aortic arch and connects the pulmonary arteries to the descending aorta during fetal development.
explanation.whyCorrect: The ductus arteriosus develops from the left 6th aortic arch. It connects the pulmonary arteries to the descending aorta, shunting most of the blood ejected from the RV directly into the aorta, bypassing the nonfunctional fetal lungs.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The left 4th aortic arch gives rise to the aortic arch itself, not the ductus arteriosus. The DA does not connect to the pulmonary veins or the ascending aorta.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The ductus arteriosus is derived from the left (not right) 6th aortic arch and connects to the descending aorta, not the ascending aorta.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The left 3rd aortic arch contributes to the common carotid arteries, not the ductus arteriosus. The DA does not directly connect the right ventricle to the aorta.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The truncus arteriosus is divided by the aorticopulmonary septum into the aorta and pulmonary artery. The ductus arteriosus is a separate structure derived from the 6th aortic arch.

questions.stem: A 14-month-old girl is being followed for a small atrial septal defect measuring 6 mm in diameter, discovered incidentally at 3 months of age. She remains asymptomatic with normal growth. Her parents ask about the need for surgical intervention. Which of the following is the most appropriate counseling regarding management of this defect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Immediate surgical closure is recommended to prevent Eisenmenger syndrome

choices.label: B
choices.text: Percutaneous closure should be performed now to prevent future arrhythmias

choices.label: C
