choices.text: The defect has a high likelihood of spontaneous closure and should be monitored

choices.label: D
choices.text: Anticoagulation therapy should be started to prevent paradoxical embolism

choices.label: E
choices.text: Prophylactic pulmonary vasodilators should be initiated to prevent hypertension

explanation.summary: Small ASDs less than 8 mm in diameter close spontaneously in nearly 80% of cases by 15 months of age. Monitoring is appropriate for small, asymptomatic defects.
explanation.whyCorrect: Defects less than 8 mm in diameter close on their own in nearly 80% of cases by 15 months of age. Since this 6 mm defect is small and the patient is asymptomatic with no significant shunting, monitoring with periodic follow-up is the most appropriate approach.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Immediate surgical closure is not indicated for small, asymptomatic ASDs with a high likelihood of spontaneous closure. Eisenmenger syndrome is a complication of large, uncorrected defects.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Percutaneous closure is reserved for larger defects or those with significant shunting. A 6 mm asymptomatic defect has a high chance of closing spontaneously.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Anticoagulation is not routinely recommended for small ASDs in infants. This therapy carries risks and is not standard management for this presentation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Prophylactic pulmonary vasodilators are not indicated in a small, asymptomatic ASD. Pulmonary hypertension is a complication of large, longstanding uncorrected defects.

questions.stem: A 3-day-old male neonate born at 38 weeks gestation is noted to have a heart murmur on routine examination. The murmur was not present at the initial newborn examination 24 hours after birth. On auscultation, a holosystolic murmur is heard at the left lower sternal border. The infant is feeding well and is acyanotic. Which of the following physiologic changes best explains why this murmur was not heard immediately after birth?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The ductus arteriosus was still patent at birth and masked the murmur sounds

choices.label: B
choices.text: The foramen ovale was still open and diverted blood flow away from the defect

choices.label: C
choices.text: Neonatal polycythemia at birth increased blood viscosity and reduced turbulent flow

choices.label: D
choices.text: Elevated pulmonary vascular resistance at birth minimized the left-to-right pressure gradient

choices.label: E
choices.text: Left ventricular contractility was depressed during the transitional circulation period

explanation.summary: VSD murmurs may not be audible immediately after birth because elevated pulmonary vascular resistance keeps RV pressures high, reducing the LV-to-RV pressure gradient and minimizing left-to-right shunting.
explanation.whyCorrect: At birth, PVR is still high, leading to elevated RV pressures. This reduces the pressure gradient between the LV and RV, decreasing left-to-right shunting across the VSD. As PVR naturally drops in the days after birth, the pressure gradient increases, producing more shunting and an audible murmur.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While the ductus arteriosus may be patent at birth, it does not mask a VSD murmur. The absence of the murmur is due to the high PVR reducing the shunt gradient, not acoustic masking.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The foramen ovale allows right-to-left flow in utero but its patency does not divert blood away from a VSD in a way that would eliminate the murmur. The key factor is PVR.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Neonatal polycythemia is not a recognized cause of absent VSD murmurs. The critical factor is the elevated PVR reducing the pressure gradient across the defect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: LV contractility is not depressed in the transitional period in a normal neonate. The delayed murmur is due to the physiologic decline in PVR that occurs after birth.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old girl with a large, uncorrected ventricular septal defect presents with worsening dyspnea, cyanosis, and exercise intolerance. Echocardiography reveals severe pulmonary hypertension with right ventricular hypertrophy and evidence of right-to-left shunting across the VSD. Which of the following is the most common initial complication that leads to the development of this clinical picture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Left atrial dilation causing mitral regurgitation and elevated pulmonary venous pressure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Infective endocarditis at the margins of the ventricular septal defect

choices.label: C
choices.text: Spontaneous partial closure of the defect creating a restrictive high-velocity jet

choices.label: D
choices.text: Chronic anemia reducing oxygen carrying capacity and worsening tissue hypoxia

choices.label: E
choices.text: Right heart failure from chronic volume and pressure overload of the right ventricle

explanation.summary: The most common complication of VSD is right heart failure due to elevated pulmonary arterial pressures from increased flow through the left-to-right shunt, which can progress to Eisenmenger syndrome.
explanation.whyCorrect: The most common complication of a VSD is right heart failure. Increased pulmonary blood flow from the left-to-right shunt leads to higher pulmonary arterial pressures. Over time, pulmonary vascular remodeling causes RV hypertrophy and eventually shunt reversal (Eisenmenger syndrome) with cyanosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While LA dilation can occur due to volume overload from the shunt returning to the left heart, mitral regurgitation is not the primary mechanism leading to Eisenmenger syndrome in VSD.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Infective endocarditis is a recognized but uncommon complication of VSD. It does not cause the progressive pulmonary hypertension and shunt reversal described.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Spontaneous partial closure would reduce shunting and improve hemodynamics, not lead to worsening pulmonary hypertension and Eisenmenger syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Chronic anemia is not a typical complication of VSD and does not explain the development of pulmonary hypertension, RV hypertrophy, and shunt reversal.

questions.stem: A 2-month-old male infant is brought to the emergency department after his mother noticed he turned blue while crying. On examination, the infant is irritable, cyanotic, and tachypneic. Heart rate is 170/min and oxygen saturation is 72%. A systolic ejection murmur is heard at the left upper sternal border, and a single S2 is noted. After initial stabilization, the infant's mother holds him with his knees pressed toward his chest, and his color gradually improves. Which of the following best explains the mechanism by which this maneuver improves the patient's symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Compression of the femoral veins increases venous return and right ventricular preload

choices.label: B
choices.text: Activation of the vagal reflex slows heart rate and reduces myocardial oxygen consumption

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased systemic vascular resistance decreases right-to-left shunting across the defect

choices.label: D
choices.text: Improved diaphragmatic excursion increases tidal volume and oxygen absorption in the lungs

choices.label: E
choices.text: Compression of the inferior vena cava reduces right atrial volume overload and congestion

explanation.summary: In tetralogy of Fallot, squatting or knee-to-chest positioning increases systemic vascular resistance, which raises LV pressure and decreases the amount of deoxygenated blood crossing right-to-left through the VSD.
explanation.whyCorrect: Bringing the knees to the chest (or squatting) compresses the femoral arteries and increases systemic vascular resistance (SVR). The increased SVR raises LV and aortic pressures, reducing the pressure gradient favoring right-to-left shunting across the VSD, thereby decreasing cyanosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While increased venous return could theoretically help, the primary mechanism of improvement is increasing systemic arterial resistance, not venous return. The clinical benefit comes from reducing right-to-left shunting.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Vagal reflex activation is not the primary mechanism by which knee-to-chest positioning improves tet spells. The improvement is hemodynamic, through increased SVR reducing shunting.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Knee-to-chest positioning does not improve diaphragmatic excursion or tidal volume. The mechanism is cardiovascular, not pulmonary.
whyWrongByChoice.E: IVC compression would reduce venous return and preload, which would be counterproductive. The benefit is from increased systemic arterial resistance.

questions.stem: A newborn infant in the well-baby nursery develops cyanosis during feeding on the first day of life. Physical examination reveals a systolic ejection murmur at the left upper sternal border and a single S2. Chest X-ray reveals a narrow mediastinum with a cardiac silhouette described as boot-shaped. Which of the following embryologic abnormalities is the primary cause of the constellation of defects seen in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Failure of the truncoconal septum to form, resulting in a single arterial outflow tract

choices.label: B
choices.text: Malalignment of the conal septum causing outflow tract stenosis and aortic displacement

choices.label: C
choices.text: Failure of the aorticopulmonary septum to spiral during cardiac development

choices.label: D
choices.text: Absence of the tricuspid valve due to failure of the endocardial cushion development

choices.label: E
choices.text: Abnormal drainage of pulmonary veins into the right atrium instead of the left atrium

explanation.summary: Tetralogy of Fallot is caused by malalignment of the conal septum, which leads to RVOT stenosis, an overriding aorta, persistent VSD, and compensatory RV hypertrophy.
explanation.whyCorrect: TOF results from malalignment of the conal septum. This malalignment causes the outflow tract to stenose (pulmonic stenosis), the aorta to override the VSD, and the VSD to persist. RV hypertrophy develops as a compensatory response to the RVOT obstruction.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Failure of the truncoconal septum to form describes persistent truncus arteriosus, not TOF. PTA results in a single outflow tract rather than the four separate defects seen in TOF.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Failure of the aorticopulmonary septum to spiral describes the embryologic basis of transposition of the great arteries, not tetralogy of Fallot.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Absence of the tricuspid valve describes tricuspid atresia, a different cyanotic CHD. TOF involves four specific defects related to conal septum malalignment.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Abnormal pulmonary venous drainage into the RA describes total anomalous pulmonary venous return (TAPVR), not TOF.

questions.stem: A 1-day-old neonate born at 39 weeks gestation becomes profoundly cyanotic shortly after birth. Pulse oximetry reveals oxygen saturation of 55%. Physical examination reveals tachypnea without grunting or retractions. Echocardiography reveals the aorta arising from the right ventricle and the pulmonary artery arising from the left ventricle, with a small patent foramen ovale providing the only communication between the two circulations. Which of the following is the most appropriate emergent intervention?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prostaglandin E1 infusion followed by balloon atrial septostomy to increase mixing

choices.label: B
choices.text: Immediate arterial switch operation to restore normal great vessel orientation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Emergent surgical creation of a systemic-to-pulmonary artery shunt for palliation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Indomethacin administration to close the foramen ovale and redirect blood flow

choices.label: E
choices.text: Extracorporeal membrane oxygenation to provide temporary cardiopulmonary support

explanation.summary: In transposition of the great arteries with inadequate mixing, emergent management includes PGE1 to maintain ductal patency and balloon atrial septostomy to enlarge the foramen ovale for greater mixing of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood.
explanation.whyCorrect: TGA is incompatible with life unless mixing of blood occurs between the two parallel circulations. PGE1 keeps the ductus arteriosus open, and balloon atrial septostomy enlarges the foramen ovale to allow greater mixing. Definitive arterial switch surgery is performed once the patient is stabilized, ideally within the first week.

whyWrongByChoice.B: While the arterial switch operation is the definitive treatment, it is not performed emergently without stabilization. The patient must first be stabilized with PGE1 and septostomy to improve mixing and oxygenation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A systemic-to-pulmonary shunt is used for right-sided obstructive lesions like TOF, not for TGA where the issue is parallel circulations without adequate mixing.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Indomethacin would close the PDA, which would worsen the situation by eliminating one of the few communications between the two circulations. The goal is to increase mixing, not reduce it.
whyWrongByChoice.E: ECMO may be considered as a rescue therapy but is not the first-line emergent intervention. PGE1 and balloon septostomy are the standard initial management.

questions.stem: A 5-day-old neonate with tricuspid atresia, a small ventricular septal defect, and pulmonary stenosis has been stable on prostaglandin E1 infusion since birth. The neonatologist explains to the parents that without the prostaglandin infusion, the infant's condition would rapidly deteriorate. Which of the following is the primary reason prostaglandin E1 is essential in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It maintains the foramen ovale opening to allow right-to-left atrial shunting

choices.label: B
choices.text: It dilates the pulmonary vasculature to overcome the pulmonary stenosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: It increases left ventricular contractility to maintain adequate systemic perfusion

choices.label: D
choices.text: It prevents closure of the ductus arteriosus which is the sole pathway to the lungs

choices.label: E
choices.text: It reduces systemic vascular resistance to improve overall cardiac output

explanation.summary: In tricuspid atresia with restricted pulmonary blood flow (small VSD and pulmonary stenosis), the ductus arteriosus is the sole pathway for blood to reach the pulmonary circulation. PGE1 keeps the ductus open.
explanation.whyCorrect: When tricuspid atresia is accompanied by a small VSD and pulmonary stenosis, the left-to-right shunt to the pulmonary circulation is severely restricted. The ductus arteriosus becomes the sole pathway for blood to enter the lungs. PGE1 infusion prevents ductal closure, which would otherwise be fatal.

whyWrongByChoice.A: PGE1 does not directly maintain the foramen ovale. While the foramen ovale is essential for survival in tricuspid atresia, PGE1 acts on the ductus arteriosus, not the foramen ovale.
whyWrongByChoice.B: PGE1 does not directly dilate the stenotic pulmonary valve or overcome pulmonary stenosis. Its role is to maintain patency of the ductus arteriosus as an alternative pathway to the lungs.
whyWrongByChoice.C: PGE1 does not increase LV contractility. Its primary cardiovascular effect in this context is maintaining ductal patency to ensure pulmonary blood flow.
whyWrongByChoice.E: PGE1 does not primarily reduce systemic vascular resistance. Its critical role here is maintaining the ductus arteriosus as the lifeline for pulmonary perfusion.

questions.stem: A 3-week-old infant presents with mild cyanosis that has been progressively worsening since the second day of life. Echocardiography reveals absence of the tricuspid valve, a hypoplastic right ventricle, a large atrial septal defect, and a small ventricular septal defect. On physical examination, a single S1, a single S2, and a holosystolic murmur at the lower left sternal border are heard. The liver is enlarged and pulsatile. Which of the following best explains the hepatomegaly in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Left ventricular failure from supplying both systemic and pulmonary circulations

choices.label: B
choices.text: Congestive hepatopathy from chronic systemic hypoxemia and tissue ischemia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Portal hypertension from congenital hepatic fibrosis associated with the cardiac defect

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hepatic congestion from pulmonary venous obstruction causing elevated left atrial pressure

choices.label: E
choices.text: Right atrial dilation from volume overload causes backflow pressure into the hepatic veins

explanation.summary: In tricuspid atresia, blood cannot pass from the RA to the RV directly. The RA dilates as blood must shunt through the ASD. This backflow pressure is transmitted to the hepatic veins, causing a large, pulsatile liver.
explanation.whyCorrect: With no functional tricuspid valve, the RA becomes dilated from volume overload as all systemic venous return must be redirected through the ASD. The elevated RA pressure is transmitted retrograde into the hepatic veins, causing hepatomegaly with a pulsatile liver.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While the LV does supply both circulations in tricuspid atresia, the pulsatile hepatomegaly is due to RA congestion and backflow pressure, not LV failure.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Congestive hepatopathy from hypoxemia alone would not produce a pulsatile liver. The pulsatile nature indicates direct venous congestion from elevated right atrial pressure.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Congenital hepatic fibrosis is not an associated finding of tricuspid atresia. The hepatomegaly is from hemodynamic congestion, not structural liver disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pulmonary venous obstruction is not a feature of tricuspid atresia. The hepatomegaly results from right-sided congestion, not elevated left atrial pressure.

questions.stem: A newborn infant is noted to have oxygen saturation of 88% on routine pulse oximetry screening. Physical examination reveals bounding pulses, a loud single S2 with an ejection click, and a diastolic murmur. Genetic testing reveals a 22q11 deletion. Which of the following congenital heart defects is most strongly associated with this genetic finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Transposition of the great arteries with intact ventricular septum

choices.label: B
choices.text: Total anomalous pulmonary venous return with pulmonary venous obstruction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Persistent truncus arteriosus with concurrent ventricular septal defect

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tricuspid atresia with restrictive ventricular septal defect

choices.label: E
choices.text: Tetralogy of Fallot with absent pulmonary valve syndrome

explanation.summary: Persistent truncus arteriosus is strongly associated with 22q11 deletion syndromes (DiGeorge syndrome, velocardiofacial syndrome). About 35% of patients with PTA have DiGeorge syndrome.
explanation.whyCorrect: Persistent truncus arteriosus is associated with 22q11 deletion syndromes, with approximately 35% of PTA patients having DiGeorge syndrome. The physical findings of bounding pulses, a loud single S2 with ejection click, and diastolic murmur (from truncal valve regurgitation) are consistent with PTA.

whyWrongByChoice.A: TGA is not specifically associated with 22q11 deletions. It results from failure of the aorticopulmonary septum to spiral, not from neural crest cell migration failure.
whyWrongByChoice.B: TAPVR is not associated with 22q11 deletions. It involves abnormal pulmonary venous drainage and has different genetic associations.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Tricuspid atresia is not specifically associated with 22q11 deletion syndromes. The physical exam findings described are more consistent with truncus arteriosus.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While TOF also involves neural crest cell abnormalities, the specific association with 22q11 deletion is much stronger for persistent truncus arteriosus, and the exam findings (single S2 with ejection click, diastolic murmur) are more consistent with PTA.

questions.stem: A 4-month-old infant with tetralogy of Fallot presents to the emergency department during an episode of intense cyanosis after prolonged crying. The infant is tachypneic with oxygen saturation of 65%. After positioning the infant with knees to chest and administering oxygen, the cyanosis persists. Which of the following medications is most appropriate for further acute management of this tet spell?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Intravenous furosemide to reduce pulmonary congestion and improve gas exchange

choices.label: B
choices.text: Morphine for sedation and propranolol to relax the outflow tract obstruction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inhaled nitric oxide to selectively dilate pulmonary arteries and increase blood flow

choices.label: D
choices.text: Intravenous milrinone to improve right ventricular contractility and pulmonary output

choices.label: E
choices.text: Intravenous phenylephrine to increase systemic vascular resistance and reduce shunting

explanation.summary: During tet spells, after positioning and oxygen, morphine is used for sedation and pain relief, and propranolol is used to relax the RVOT obstruction and promote pulmonary blood flow.
explanation.whyCorrect: Morphine is used during tet spells for sedation and to reduce the agitation that worsens RVOT spasm. Propranolol relaxes the RVOT obstruction and promotes pulmonary blood flow. These are established medical therapies for acute tet spell management.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Furosemide reduces volume, which would decrease preload and worsen the tet spell. The problem in TOF is RVOT obstruction causing right-to-left shunting, not pulmonary congestion.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Inhaled nitric oxide is not standard management for tet spells. The issue is mechanical RVOT obstruction, not pulmonary vasoconstriction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Milrinone is a vasodilator and inotrope that could lower SVR, potentially worsening the right-to-left shunt. It is not used in acute tet spell management.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While increasing SVR can help reduce right-to-left shunting, phenylephrine is not the standard first-line pharmacologic therapy for tet spells. Morphine and propranolol directly address agitation and RVOT spasm.

questions.stem: A neonatologist is counseling parents of a newborn diagnosed with transposition of the great arteries. The parents ask about the long-term prognosis. The neonatologist explains that without surgical intervention, this condition carries an extremely poor prognosis, but with appropriate treatment, outcomes are favorable. Which of the following best describes the expected survival data for this condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Without treatment, 50% survive past age 3; with surgery, 95% survive to age 10

choices.label: B
choices.text: Without treatment, 30% survive the first year; with surgery, 80% survive to age 10

choices.label: C
choices.text: Without treatment, 15% survive at 1 year; with surgery, 90% survive past 10 years

choices.label: D
choices.text: Without treatment, 10% survive the first year; with surgery, 90% survive to age 20

choices.label: E
choices.text: Without treatment, 80% survive the first year; with surgery, 99% survive to adulthood

explanation.summary: Without treatment for TGA, only 10% of patients survive the first year of life. After arterial switch operation, the long-term survival rate is 90% at age 20 years.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material states that without treatment, only 10% of patients with TGA survive the first year of life. However, after treatment (arterial switch operation), the long-term survival rate is 90% at age 20 years.

whyWrongByChoice.A: These survival statistics (50% past age 3 without treatment) more closely match tetralogy of Fallot, not TGA. TGA has a much worse natural history with only 10% first-year survival.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The untreated survival rate of 30% is too high for TGA, and the treated survival rate of 80% at 10 years is lower than the actual reported 90% at 20 years.
whyWrongByChoice.C: These statistics (15% at 1 year untreated, 90% past 10 years treated) more closely match persistent truncus arteriosus, not TGA.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An untreated first-year survival of 80% is far too optimistic for TGA. Without treatment, TGA is rapidly fatal with only 10% surviving the first year.

questions.stem: A 2-day-old neonate in the NICU has progressive cyanosis with oxygen saturations declining from 92% to 78% over the past 12 hours. Echocardiography reveals absence of the tricuspid valve, a large ASD, and a moderate VSD with unobstructed pulmonary outflow. Which of the following best explains the progressive worsening of cyanosis in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The ventricular septal defect is undergoing spontaneous closure in the neonatal period

choices.label: B
choices.text: The atrial septal defect is becoming more restrictive as left atrial pressure rises

choices.label: C
choices.text: The ductus arteriosus is beginning to close, reducing an alternative source of pulmonary blood flow

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dropping pulmonary vascular resistance is causing excessive pulmonary blood flow and LV overload

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increasing pulmonary vascular resistance after birth is restricting pulmonary blood flow

explanation.summary: In tricuspid atresia, even with a moderate VSD and unobstructed pulmonary flow, the ductus arteriosus provides an important supplementary pathway to the lungs. As the DA begins to close around the second day of life, pulmonary blood flow decreases and cyanosis worsens.
explanation.whyCorrect: The ductus arteriosus normally begins to close functionally by 15-30 hours after birth. In tricuspid atresia, the DA contributes to pulmonary blood flow. As it closes on the second day of life, the cyanosis worsens because one source of pulmonary perfusion is being lost.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Spontaneous VSD closure does not occur this rapidly in the neonatal period. Small to moderate VSDs may close over months to years, not within the first days of life.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The ASD is described as large, and there is no indication it is becoming restrictive. In tricuspid atresia, the ASD is essential for survival and typically remains patent.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Dropping PVR would actually increase pulmonary blood flow, which in tricuspid atresia with unobstructed pulmonary outflow would cause heart failure symptoms rather than worsening cyanosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: PVR normally decreases after birth, not increases. The worsening cyanosis on day 2 is temporally consistent with ductal closure, not rising PVR.

questions.stem: A 10-day-old infant presents with tachypnea, poor feeding, and mild cyanosis. Echocardiography reveals the pulmonary veins draining into the superior vena cava via the brachiocephalic vein instead of the left atrium. A moderate ASD is present, providing the only route for oxygenated blood to reach the systemic circulation. Which of the following physical examination findings would be expected if this patient develops progressive right heart failure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Right ventricular heave, loud pulmonic component of S2, peripheral edema, and hepatomegaly

choices.label: B
choices.text: Laterally displaced apical impulse, bibasilar crackles, and an S3 gallop

choices.label: C
choices.text: Narrow pulse pressure, pulsus paradoxus, and distant muffled heart sounds

choices.label: D
choices.text: Harsh pansystolic murmur at the apex with radiation to the axilla and bilateral wheezes

choices.label: E
choices.text: Bounding peripheral pulses, wide pulse pressure, and a continuous machinery murmur

explanation.summary: Total anomalous pulmonary venous return causes right heart volume overload. Progressive RV failure manifests as RV heave, loud P2, peripheral edema, and hepatomegaly.
explanation.whyCorrect: In TAPVR, all pulmonary venous blood returns to the right heart, causing volume overload. Progressive RV failure and hypertrophy manifest as an RV heave, a loud pulmonic component of S2 (P2) from pulmonary hypertension, peripheral edema, and hepatomegaly.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A laterally displaced apical impulse and S3 gallop are signs of left ventricular failure, not right heart failure. In TAPVR, the primary pathology affects the right side of the heart.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Narrow pulse pressure, pulsus paradoxus, and muffled heart sounds suggest cardiac tamponade, not right heart failure from TAPVR.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A pansystolic murmur at the apex radiating to the axilla suggests mitral regurgitation, which is not a feature of TAPVR or its right heart failure complications.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Bounding pulses, wide pulse pressure, and a continuous machinery murmur are characteristic of patent ductus arteriosus, not TAPVR.

questions.stem: A 3-day-old neonate with persistent truncus arteriosus and a ventricular septal defect is being stabilized in the NICU. The attending neonatologist specifically avoids administering supplemental oxygen. A medical student asks why oxygen is being withheld in a cyanotic infant. Which of the following best explains the rationale for avoiding supplemental oxygen in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Oxygen may promote premature closure of the ductus arteriosus needed for pulmonary flow

choices.label: B
choices.text: High oxygen concentrations increase the risk of retinopathy of prematurity in this neonate

choices.label: C
choices.text: Oxygen therapy causes bronchopulmonary dysplasia when administered in the first week of life

choices.label: D
choices.text: Supplemental oxygen promotes reactive oxygen species that damage the truncal valve leaflets

choices.label: E
choices.text: Supplemental oxygen may be harmful by excessively dilating pulmonary vessels and worsening the shunt

explanation.summary: In persistent truncus arteriosus, supplementary oxygen may be harmful because it can excessively dilate pulmonary vasculature, increasing pulmonary blood flow at the expense of systemic circulation, worsening the hemodynamic imbalance.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material specifically states that supplementary oxygen may be harmful for patients with persistent truncus arteriosus. Oxygen dilates pulmonary vessels, which increases pulmonary blood flow from the common trunk at the expense of systemic perfusion, worsening the overall hemodynamic balance.

whyWrongByChoice.A: In PTA, there is a single outflow tract, so the ductus arteriosus is not the primary pathway for pulmonary flow. The concern is not about ductal closure but about pulmonary vasodilation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Retinopathy of prematurity is a concern in premature infants, but the question describes a term neonate with a specific cardiac defect. This is not the reason oxygen is avoided.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Bronchopulmonary dysplasia is related to prolonged oxygen exposure in premature infants. This is not the reason for avoiding supplemental oxygen in PTA management.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Reactive oxygen species damaging truncal valve leaflets is not a recognized concern. The harm from oxygen in PTA is hemodynamic, related to pulmonary vasodilation.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant with known tetralogy of Fallot has not yet undergone surgical repair. The parents report that the child frequently squats during play. On examination, the child has cyanosis and early clubbing of the fingers and toes. The systolic ejection murmur at the left upper sternal border is noted to be softer than on previous visits. Which of the following best explains the change in murmur intensity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Development of tricuspid regurgitation is diverting flow away from the outflow tract

choices.label: B
choices.text: Progressive right ventricular dilation is enlarging the VSD and reducing the shunt velocity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increasing right ventricular hypertrophy is improving the efficiency of RV ejection

choices.label: D
choices.text: Worsening RVOT obstruction is causing more blood to shunt right-to-left across the VSD

choices.label: E
choices.text: Worsening pulmonary valve stenosis is reducing turbulent flow through the RVOT

explanation.summary: In TOF, the systolic ejection murmur is caused by turbulent flow through the RVOT, not the VSD. As RVOT obstruction worsens, less blood flows through the RVOT and more is diverted right-to-left across the VSD, making the murmur softer.
explanation.whyCorrect: The murmur in TOF is generated by turbulent flow through the RVOT obstruction. As the obstruction worsens, less blood flows through the RVOT (more is shunted right-to-left across the VSD instead), resulting in less turbulence and a softer murmur. The worsening cyanosis and squatting behavior support increasing right-to-left shunting.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Tricuspid regurgitation is not a typical progressive complication of TOF. The softer murmur is explained by increased right-to-left shunting across the VSD.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Progressive RV dilation enlarging the VSD is not the mechanism. The VSD size is anatomically fixed. The change is due to altered flow dynamics from worsening RVOT obstruction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: RV hypertrophy is compensatory but does not improve ejection efficiency in the setting of worsening outflow obstruction. The softer murmur indicates less flow through the RVOT.
whyWrongByChoice.E: This answer is partially correct about the mechanism but incorrect in reasoning. Worsening stenosis does not reduce turbulence per se; rather, it diverts more blood across the VSD, reducing total flow through the RVOT.

questions.stem: A newborn infant with total anomalous pulmonary venous return and obstructed pulmonary veins is in severe respiratory distress with an oxygen saturation of 60% on room air. The neonatologist immediately begins prostaglandin E1 infusion. Which of the following is the primary rationale for this urgent intervention?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To dilate the obstructed pulmonary veins and improve blood return to the left atrium

choices.label: B
choices.text: To reduce pulmonary vascular resistance and improve oxygenation at the alveolar level

choices.label: C
choices.text: To keep the ductus arteriosus patent until surgical correction can be performed

choices.label: D
choices.text: To increase left ventricular contractility and maintain adequate systemic perfusion

choices.label: E
choices.text: To prevent closure of the foramen ovale and maintain interatrial communication

explanation.summary: In TAPVR, the ductus arteriosus closes functionally within 15-30 hours after birth. Early PGE1 infusion keeps it open, providing a lifesaving right-to-left shunt until surgical correction is performed.
explanation.whyCorrect: The ductus arteriosus normally closes 15-30 hours after birth. In TAPVR, keeping the DA patent with PGE1 infusion is lifesaving because it allows some oxygenated blood to reach the systemic circulation via the right-to-left shunt through the ductus while awaiting definitive surgical repair.

whyWrongByChoice.A: PGE1 does not dilate pulmonary veins. The obstruction in TAPVR is an anatomic abnormality requiring surgical correction, not pharmacologic vasodilation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: PGE1 does not directly reduce PVR or improve alveolar oxygenation. Its role is to maintain ductal patency for systemic perfusion.
whyWrongByChoice.D: PGE1 does not increase LV contractility. Its critical action in TAPVR is maintaining patency of the ductus arteriosus.
whyWrongByChoice.E: PGE1 does not directly maintain the foramen ovale. While the foramen ovale is important for survival in TAPVR, PGE1 acts specifically on the ductus arteriosus.

questions.stem: A medical genetics team is evaluating a neonate with a cyanotic congenital heart defect. The cardiologist notes that the embryologic basis of this defect involves failure of neural crest cells to migrate to the area of the truncus arteriosus, preventing formation of the septum that normally divides it into two separate vessels. A concurrent Hirschsprung disease is also suspected based on abdominal distension and failure to pass meconium. Which of the following cardiac conditions shares the same embryologic neural crest cell migration defect as this patient's heart disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tetralogy of Fallot due to malalignment of the conal septum with neural crest involvement

choices.label: B
choices.text: Atrial septal defect due to failure of septum primum and septum secundum fusion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Transposition of the great arteries due to failure of aorticopulmonary septum spiraling

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tricuspid atresia due to absence of tricuspid valve formation from endocardial cushions

choices.label: E
choices.text: Total anomalous pulmonary venous return due to abnormal pulmonary vein development

explanation.summary: Both persistent truncus arteriosus and tetralogy of Fallot result from failure of neural crest cell migration. In PTA, neural crest cells fail to reach the truncus arteriosus. In TOF, abnormal neural crest cell migration contributes to the VSD and overriding aorta.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material states that both TOF and PTA result from failure of neural crest cell migration, and notes that Hirschsprung disease is another condition caused by neural crest cell migration failure. In TOF, the overriding aorta and VSD involve abnormal migration of neural crest cells.

whyWrongByChoice.B: ASD results from defects in septum primum or septum secundum formation, which is not related to neural crest cell migration.
whyWrongByChoice.C: TGA results from failure of the aorticopulmonary septum to spiral properly. This is a different embryologic mechanism from neural crest cell migration failure.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Tricuspid atresia involves absence of the tricuspid valve and is related to abnormal development of the atrioventricular structures, not neural crest cell migration.
whyWrongByChoice.E: TAPVR involves abnormal pulmonary venous connections and is not related to neural crest cell migration failure.
